name: Dhul-Qarnayn
description: Dhul-Qarnayn, meaning "The Two-Horned One," is a figure mentioned in the Quran, specifically in Surah Al-Kahf (The Cave). He is depicted as a righteous and powerful king granted immense authority and wisdom by Allah. Dhul-Qarnayn travels extensively, venturing to the east and west, encountering various peoples and civilizations. He is known for building a massive iron wall to protect a vulnerable community from the destructive tribes of Gog and Magog (Ya'juj and Ma'juj), who are described as savage and chaotic. His journeys are marked by justice and the establishment of order. He consistently attributes his successes to Allah's grace and guidance, exemplifying piety and humility despite his great power. The identity of Dhul-Qarnayn is debated, with some scholars identifying him with figures such as Alexander the Great, Cyrus the Great, or a pre-Islamic Arabian king. Regardless of his historical identity, Dhul-Qarnayn serves as a symbol of righteous leadership, divine blessing, and the ultimate power of faith and obedience to God. He represents a just ruler who used his authority to protect the weak and establish peace, all while remaining steadfast in his belief and acknowledging Allah as the source of his strength. His story serves as a reminder of the impermanence of worldly power and the importance of using one's gifts for good.
subtitle: The Two-Horned One: Righteous King

keyMyths.title: Journey to the East
keyMyths.description: Dhul-Qarnayn traveled to the east and found a people who were exposed to the scorching sun. Allah gave him the power to either punish them or treat them with kindness.

keyMyths.title: Journey to the West
keyMyths.description: Dhul-Qarnayn traveled to the west and reached a spring of murky water, where he found a people. Allah gave him the power to either punish them or treat them with kindness.

keyMyths.title: The Wall Against Gog and Magog
keyMyths.description: Dhul-Qarnayn built a wall of iron and molten copper to protect a community from the destructive tribes of Gog and Magog. This wall will remain until the appointed time by Allah.

sources.author: Various Translators
sources.description: Primary source for the story of Dhul-Qarnayn.
sources.text: Primary source for the story of Dhul-Qarnayn.

sources.author: Ibn Kathir
sources.description: Comprehensive commentary on the Quran, including interpretations of Dhul-Qarnayn's story.
sources.text: Comprehensive commentary on the Quran, including interpretations of Dhul-Qarnayn's story.

associations.name: Horns
associations.significance: Represent power, authority, and possibly divine favor. Interpretations vary from literal horns to metaphorical representations of reaching the 'two horns' (ends) of the earth or possessing two eras of power.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Dhul-Qarnayn is portrayed as a just ruler who uses his power to help those in need and establish order, reflecting the Islamic ideal of a righteous leader.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: He is blessed with divine wisdom and understanding, enabling him to make sound judgments and navigate complex situations.

associations.name: Iron Wall
associations.significance: Symbolizes protection and defense against chaos and evil, representing the power of faith and divine assistance in overcoming threats.

associations.name: Travels
associations.significance: Represents the pursuit of knowledge, exploration of the world, and the spread of justice and divine message.

associations.name: East and West
associations.significance: Signifies Dhul-Qarnayn's dominion over the entire world, reaching the farthest corners of the earth.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both figures are associated with extensive conquests, legendary status, and divine connections. Some scholars believe Dhul-Qarnayn is a reference to Alexander.
Cyrus is also proposed as a possible inspiration, due to his just rule, vast empire, and building projects.
The wall-building narrative has parallels with legends of a messianic figure who will protect the righteous from Gog and Magog.

cultural.modernLegacy: Dhul-Qarnayn remains a popular figure in Islamic culture, literature, and art, often cited as an example of a just and divinely guided ruler. His story continues to inspire discussions about leadership, justice, and the balance between faith and worldly power. He is also a subject of theological debate regarding his identity and the nature of his wall.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Divine Mandate
extendedContent.content: The Quran does not provide specific details about Dhul-Qarnayn's birth or early life. He appears in the narrative already possessing great power and wisdom, which are explicitly stated to be gifts from Allah. He is portrayed as a devout monotheist who uses his authority to uphold justice and spread righteousness. The name 'Dhul-Qarnayn,' meaning 'The Two-Horned One,' has led to various interpretations. Some scholars suggest it refers to a helmet adorned with horns, symbolizing power, while others interpret it metaphorically, representing his dominion over the 'two horns' (east and west) of the earth or two distinct eras of his reign. Regardless of the literal meaning, the name signifies his unique position as a divinely appointed ruler with exceptional capabilities. His initial actions demonstrate a commitment to fairness and a desire to guide people towards the right path, establishing him as a benevolent leader.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and the Iron Wall
extendedContent.content: Dhul-Qarnayn's most notable quest involves his journey to the east and west, where he encounters various peoples and civilizations. In the west, he reaches a place where the sun sets in a murky spring, and he judges the people he finds there, rewarding the righteous and punishing the wicked. In the east, he finds a people who are vulnerable to the attacks of Gog and Magog, barbaric tribes who spread chaos and destruction. Upon their request, Dhul-Qarnayn constructs a massive wall made of iron and molten copper to protect them. This wall serves as a barrier against Gog and Magog, preventing them from ravaging the land. The construction of the iron wall is a testament to Dhul-Qarnayn's engineering skills, resourcefulness, and divine assistance. It symbolizes the power of faith and righteous action in overcoming evil and protecting the vulnerable. The wall is prophesied to remain until the end times, when it will be broken, signaling the approach of the Day of Judgment.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Influence
extendedContent.content: Dhul-Qarnayn's legacy extends beyond his military achievements and building projects. He is remembered as a model of just and righteous leadership, guided by divine wisdom. His story serves as an inspiration for rulers and individuals alike, emphasizing the importance of upholding justice, protecting the vulnerable, and using power for the benefit of humanity. The tale of Dhul-Qarnayn continues to be retold and reinterpreted in various cultural contexts, reflecting the enduring appeal of his character and the timeless themes of his story. The question of his historical identity remains a subject of scholarly debate, with various figures, such as Alexander the Great and Cyrus the Great, being proposed as possible inspirations. Regardless of his specific identity, Dhul-Qarnayn's story serves as a powerful reminder of the potential for human greatness when guided by faith and a commitment to justice. His tale encourages reflection on the responsibilities of leadership and the importance of using power to create a better world.

shortDescription: A Quranic figure, possibly inspired by Alexander the Great or Cyrus. He's known for his travels, justice, and building a wall against Gog and Magog.
